coredata.dc:title: Effect of reaction parameters on the corrosion inhibition behavior of N-doped carbon dots for metal in 1 M HCl solution
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Jiaxin
Cheng, Xiong
Zhong, Chaofa
Chen, Xinhua
Ye, Y.W.
Zhao, H.
Chen, H.
coredata.dc:description: A series of N-CDs were prepared from tryptophan under different reaction temperatures (200, 180, 160 °C) and different reaction time (0.5, 1, 2 h). Many professional devices were selected to research the inhibition behavior of N-CDs in HCl environment. These results revealed that reaction temperature and reaction time would greatly affect the corrosion behavior of steel in 1 M HCl environment. As the reaction temperature and time increased, the corrosion inhibition effect firstly enhanced and then weakened, and reached the best performance at 180 °C and 1 h. In the meantime, the icorr of steel in blank solution sharply decreased after adding N-CDs. At 200 mg/L of N-CDs, the inhibition efficiency for steel was higher than 90%. The adsorption isotherm and XPS analysis showed that the N-CDs would tightly cover on steel surface by physicochemical interaction.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-CDs
Reaction temperature
Reaction time
Inhibition efficiency

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by Scientific Research Foundation of Jiangxi University of Science and Technology (No. 205200100421); Jiangxi Provincial Department of Education ( No. GJJ190469 ); Program of Qingjiang Excellent Young Talents, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology (JXUSTQJYX2020010); Distinguished Professor Program of Jinggang Scholars in institutions of higher learning, Jiangxi Province; Jiangxi Province “Double Thousand Plan” Science and Technology Innovation high-end Talent Project (jxsq2019201039); Jiangxi Province “Double Thousand Plan” Innovation Leading Talent Short-term Project (jxsq2020007).

Science and Technology Innovation high-end Talent Project (jxsq2019201039); Jiangxi Province ?Double Thousand Plan? Innovation Leading Talent Short-term Project (jxsq2020007).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structure characterization of as-obtained inhibitor at 200 °C-0.5 h (a) FTIR, (b) UV–Vis, (c) XPS, (d) XPS-C1s, (e) XPS-N1s, (f) TEM.
EIS results after 1 day soaking (a)(b)(c) 200 °C-0.5 h, (d)(e)(f) 180 °C-0.5 h, (g)(h)(i) 160 °C-0.5 h.
Electrical equivalent circuit fitted by EIS data.
The variation of Nyquist diagrams and Bode plots as a function of reaction time after 1 day soaking in HCl solution (a)(b) 200 °C-1 h, (c)(d) 200 °C-2 h.
Potentiodynamic polarization plots of steel after 1 day soaking in HCl environment with various inhibitors (a) 200 °C-0.5 h, (b) 180 °C-1 h, (c) 160 °C-1 h, (d) 200 °C-1 h, (e) 200 °C-2 h.

SEM images of steel after 1 day soaking (a) blank specimen, (b) 50 mg/L-200 °C-0.5 h, (c) 100 mg/L-200 °C-0.5 h, (d) 150 mg/L-200 °C-0.5 h, (e) 200 mg/L-200 °C-0.5 h, (f) 200 mg/L-180 °C-0.5 h, (g) 200 mg/L-160 °C-0.5 h, (h) 200 mg/L-200 °C-1 h, (i) 200 mg/L-200 °C-2 h.

3D surface morphology of electrode after 1 day soaking (a) blank specimen, (b) 50 mg/L-200 °C-0.5 h, (c) 100 mg/L-200 °C-0.5 h, (d) 150 mg/L-200 °C-0.5 h, (e) 200 mg/L-200 °C-0.5 h, (f) 200 mg/L-180 °C-0.5 h, (g) 200 mg/L-160 °C-0.5 h, (h) 200 mg/L-200 °C-1 h, (i) 200 mg/L-200 °C-2 h.
Corrosion rate of steel after soaking under different conditions (a) reaction temperature and (b) reaction time.
Adsorption isotherm of bare steel after soaking in various solutions (a) 200 °C-0.5 h, (b) 180 °C-1 h, (c) 160 °C-1 h, (d) 200 °C-1 h, (e) 200 °C-2 h.
XPS-N1s analysis of steel surface after soaking in N-CDs solution.
Adsorption and anti-corrosion mechanism of N-CDs for steel.
The electrochemical parameters fitting in the EIS results.
The electrochemical parameters fitting in polarization curve results.

head.ce:title: Effect of reaction parameters on the corrosion inhibition behavior of N-doped carbon dots for metal in 1 M HCl solution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Materials Metallurgy and Chemistry, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology, Ganzhou, Jiangxi 341000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Materials Metallurgy and Chemistry
Jiangxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Materials Metallurgy and Chemistry, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology, Ganzhou, Jiangxi 341000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangxi University of Science and Technology, Ganzhou, Jiangxi 341000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangxi University of Science and Technology, Ganzhou, Jiangxi 341000, ChinaPlease note that the significance of footnote “**” link with the author “H. Chen” is not provided, hence it has been deleted. Please check and correct if necessary.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A series of N-CDs were prepared from tryptophan under different reaction temperatures (200, 180, 160 °C) and different reaction time (0.5, 1, 2 h). Many professional devices were selected to research the inhibition behavior of N-CDs in HCl environment. These results revealed that reaction temperature and reaction time would greatly affect the corrosion behavior of steel in 1 M HCl environment. As the reaction temperature and time increased, the corrosion inhibition effect firstly enhanced and then weakened, and reached the best performance at 180 °C and 1 h. In the meantime, the icorr of steel in blank solution sharply decreased after adding N-CDs. At 200 mg/L of N-CDs, the inhibition efficiency for steel was higher than 90%. The adsorption isotherm and XPS analysis showed that the N-CDs would tightly cover on steel surface by physicochemical interaction.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to its numerous physical properties and inexpensiveness, low carbon steel is widely used in industrial fields [1]. However, serious corrosion is prone to be caused by surrounding environment during service process of low carbon steel [2–4]. At this case, corrosion may cause the renewal of various infrastructure and equipment, and the loss of life and environmental problems, which brings huge costs [5]. It is estimated that the global corrosion cost in the world is close to $2.5 trillion, accounting for about 3.4% of the global gross domestic product (GDP) [6]. Thus, it is necessary to develop effective anti-corrosion technology to delay or prevent the damage of metallic equipment.

By using various corrosion protection methods, such as inhibitor, alloying and protective coatings, the damage of various metals can be significantly inhibited. Among all methods, corrosion inhibitors are generally used as a convenient, economical and effective protective method [7,8]. By analysis, the protective nature of corrosion inhibitors is surface adsorption, which is used to protect materials from corrosion by isolating corrosive media and then reduce the active reaction site. Some corrosion inhibitors are composed of N, P, S, and other heteroatoms. They can easily attach to the metal surface through π and non-bonded electrons, thus decreasing the contact opportunity from aggressive ion [9–11]. According to previous report, some traditional inhibitors, such as arsenates, phosphates, chromates and dichromates, presented good corrosion inhibition effect [12]. Nevertheless, the high cost and environmental pollution restrict their further application [13–15]. The development of new environmentally friendly corrosion inhibitors is imminent.

As a new type of corrosion inhibitor, carbon dots have received extensive attention in the field of corrosion protection in recent years [16–18]. For example, Cui et al. [19] synthesized two kinds of N-CDs from p-phenylenediamine and o-phenylenediamine, and the corrosion inhibition efficiency was 83.1 and 81.9% at 200 mg/L for carbon steel in HCl environment. Yang et al. [20] prepared ionic liquid functionalized N-CDs and studied its inhibitive nature on carbon steel in acid and neutral solutions, and found that the functionalized N-CDs displayed efficient protection performance in both solutions. Ye et al. [21] selected citric acid and L-histidine as raw material to prepare N-CDs, and the corrosion inhibition efficiency was up to 96% for steel in HCl solution. Cao et al. [22] prepared N-CDs from citric acid and urea, and confirmed that the corrosion inhibition efficiency for steel was 95% at 30 mg/L. Zhou et al. [23] discussed the protective function of N-CDs for copper in acid medium at various temperatures (20 ~ 60 °C), and got the lowest corrosion current density at 60 °C. Saraswat at al. [24] achieved a N-CDs inhibitor and reported that the N-CDs performed good corrosion inhibition ability for steel in acid solution.

In this work, tryptophan was chosen to prepare a high efficiency and eco-friendly inhibitor. FTIR, UV–vis, XPS, TEM technologies were selected to evaluate the structure of N-CDs. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and polarization measurement were selected to analyze the electrochemistry property of steel in acid environment with and without inhibitor. After corrosion test, SEM, LSCM and XPS were chosen to analyze the surface feature of steel. The weight loss measurement was used to calculate the corrosion rate of steel during soaking.

Q235 carbon steel, tryptophan, hydrochloric acid and ethanol were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. AB glue (3:1) was purchased from Yasong Material Co., Ltd. Before test, the steels were cut in size of 1.0 × 1.0 × 1.0 cm3 for weight loss and electrochemical measurement. After that, the steels were connected to the wire by soldering. Finally, the steels were covered by AB glue and only 1 cm2 area was exposed for test. The chemical composition of Q235 steel was 0.19 wt% C, 0.31 wt% Si, 0.45 wt% Mn, 0.03 wt% S, 0.02 wt% P and 99 wt% Fe.

N-CDs corrosion inhibitor was synthesized by high temperature cracking method with tryptophan as raw material, and the synthetic process was revealed in Fig. 1 . Firstly, 2 g of tryptophan was weighted by an electronic scale and then transferred to a beaker. Secondly, the beaker was put into the drying box, and then the temperature and time were set. The reaction parameters were 160 °C-0.5 h, 180 °C-0.5 h, 200 °C-0.5 h, 200 °C-1 h, 200 °C-2 h. After the reaction was over, 30 mL of distilled water was added to the beaker and then sonicated for 20 min, followed by successive filtration and dialysis treatments. Finally, the dialysate was dried at 65 °C for 48 h to obtain N-CDs corrosion inhibitor.

FTIR was employed to characterize the functional groups of materials. The elemental information and composition of chemical bonding were studied by XPS. The morphology of as-prepared inhibitor was obtained by TEM. The corrosion situation of steel in inhibitor solution was evaluated by electrochemical measurements (EIS and tafel curve), which was performed using an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E) with a three-electrode cell. Among them, the counter electrode was made of platinum, the saturated calomel electrode (SCE) was utilized as a reference, and the working electrode was Q235 steel with a total surface area of 1 cm2. Before the measurement, it was determined that the system reached a stable state by analyzing the stability of open circuit potential (OCP). The corrosion solutions were 1 M HCl with 0, 50, 100, 150, 200 mg/L of as-prepared N-CDs inhibitor. After immersion 1 day, the EIS tests were conducted at 100 kHz ~ 100 mHz with an amplitude sinusoidal voltage of 20 mV. Then the electrochemical parameters were fitted by ZSimpWin software. The tafel curves were obtained at a scanning rate of 0.5 mV s−1 and a potential range of − 250 to 250 mV versus SCE after 1 day immersion. The surface coverage (θ) and efficiency of inhibition (IE) were calculated from equations (1) and (2) [25,26]:
(1)
θ =
i
corr
0
-
i
corr
i
corr
0
(2)
IE =
i
corr
0
-
i
corr
i
corr
0
× 100 %
Among them, i0 corr was the current density in blank solution and icorr was the current density after adding N-CDs. After these tests, SEM and LSCM were used to analyze the corrosion situation and roughness of steel surface. The corrosion product was analyzed by XPS and the corrosion rate of steel in testing medium was acquired according to the weight loss method. The adsorption isotherm and adsorption free energy (ΔG0 ads) were obtained from equations (3) and (4) [27,28]:
(3)
θ
1 - θ
=
K
ads
C
(4)
Δ
G
ads
0
= - R T I n
1000
K
ads
Among them, θ was surface coverage, C was N-CDs concentration, and Kads represented the adsorption equilibrium constant, R was 8.314 J/mol-1K−1 and the T was 298 K.

The FTIR, UV–Vis, XPS and TEM results of as-prepared inhibitor were presented in Fig. 2 . In the case of FTIR spectrum, the peaks of absorption at 1295.9, 1436.8, 1537.9, 2836.1, 3402.5 cm−1 was made up of C-O, C-N, C = N, C-H, N-H (Fig. 2 a). Meanwhile, the stretching vibration peaks at 1647.1 and 1693.8 cm−1 were related to –CO-NH- and C = O groups [29]. For UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 2 b), the as-prepared N-CDs inhibitor showed two absorbance peaks at 280 and 345 nm, which was assigned to π-π* transition of C = O bond and n-π* transition of C = N bond, respectively [30]. XPS was performed to determine the chemical composition and bonding state of as-prepared N-CDs inhibitor. By detection, the surface survey of N-CDs showed the presence of C, N and O (Fig. 2 c). Meanwhile, the XPS-C 1 s spectrum contained three peaks at 284.6, 285.8 and 287.7 eV (Fig. 2 d), corresponding to C-C, C-N and C-O, respectively [31]. The high-resolution spectrum of N 1 s showed two characteristic peaks, which located at 398.3 and 399.9 eV, corresponding to N-H and N-C bonds (Fig. 2 e) [32]. Through TEM observation, the morphology of N-CDs was unevenly spherical, the distribution of N-CDs was uniform and the average size was about 2 ~ 3 nm (Fig. 2 f).

The EIS result was revealed in Fig. 3 . According to previous study, the anti-corrosion ability can be assessed by the capacitive arc diameter and the impedance module at low frequency in Nyquist plots [33]. After 1 day soaking, compared with Fig. S1
a, the capacitive arc diameter was enlarged obviously after adding N-CDs in 1 M HCl solution. This regular increment in diameter suggested the adsorption of N-CDs molecules on the surface of metal or the establishment of thin film from N-CDs. This protective film could reduce the active reaction area of steel, increased its corrosion resistance ability and clearly suppressed the steel corrosion in HCl solution. As N-CDs content increased, the thickness or the cover area of adsorption film was enhanced, and the protective effect was better. At the same N-CDs content, the capacitive arc diameter reached the highest at 180 °C (Fig. 3 a, d, g). For |Z|0.1Hz value, the value of steel in blank solution was about 22 Ω•cm2 ( Fig. S1
b). After adding N-CDs, the |Z|0.1Hz value showed a distinctly uptrend and the ascensional range was enlarged as N-CDs content increased. At 200 mg/L of N-CDs, the |Z|0.1Hz increased to 1000, 1350, and 1180 Ω•cm2 at 200, 180 and 160 °C, respectively (Fig. 3 b, e, h). By calculation, the |Z|0.1Hz values were 45.5, 61.36, 53.64 times higher than the result in Fig. S1
b. Thus, the corrosion situation in HCl solution could be effectively inhibited by adding N-CDs at different reaction temperatures. In the case of phase angle, the frequency range with the maximum phase angle was low ( Fig. S1
c). However, the frequency range with the maximum phase angle became larger with the increase of N-CDs content (Fig. 3 c, f, i). These results also indicated the adsorption of N-CDs molecules on steel surface and thus endowing its effective protective ability on steel [34]. By comparison, the protection effect of N-CDs prepared at 180 °C was the best.

In order to achieve a better understanding of EIS results, the electrical equivalent circuit fitted by EIS data was shown in Fig. 4 , which contained solution resistance (Rs), polarization resistance (Rp), double-layer capacitance (Cdl) and deviation index (n). From EIS data, the fitting parameters were displayed in Table 1 . Obviously, the Rs value was about 2 ~ 4 Ω•cm2 in various solutions, implying that the Rs value did not depend on N-CDs content. In the case of polarization resistance, the Rp value of steel in blank solution was about 21.6 Ω•cm2, and the value was greatly enhanced after adding N-CDs. As N-CDs content increased, the Rp value revealed an increasing trend and even increased by an order of magnitude. Under the same condition, the Rp value was the highest in HCl solution after adding N-CDs prepared at 180 °C. For double-layer capacitance, the Cdl value of steel in blank solution was about 396.4 μF•cm−2, and the value was clearly reduced after adding N-CDs. As N-CDs content increased, the Cdl value showed a decreasing trend and even decreased by an order of magnitude. Under the same condition, the Cdl value was the lowest in corrosion solution after adding N-CDs prepared at 180 °C. In terms of deviation index, the n value could use to reflect the roughness of corrosion interface. A high n value indicated a smooth corrosion interface. In blank solution, the n value was about 0.83, which was the lowest among all conditions, suggesting that the roughness of corrosion interface was the largest and the corrosion was the most serious. After adding N-CDs, the n value was enlarged and the enhancement degree was proportional to the content of N-CDs. In the meantime, the n value of steel in HCl solution after adding N-CDs prepared at 180 °C was obviously higher than other environments, implying the smoother interface roughness and weaker corrosion.

The reaction time was also a key to affect the corrosion inhibition ability of N-CDs. Fig. 5 revealed the variation of Nyquist diagrams and Bode plots as a function of reaction time. Combined with Fig. 3 a and b, the reaction time was 0.5, 1 and 2 h. In terms of Nyquist diagram, at the same N-CDs content, the capacitive arc diameter reached the highest value at 1 h (Fig. 5 a and c). In the case of Bode plots, the change trend of |Z|0.1Hz value was similar with the capacitive arc diameter (Fig. 5 b and d). When the reaction time was 1 h, the |Z|0.1Hz value reached the highest value at various N-CDs contents compared with other reaction time. Through calculation, the |Z|0.1Hz value at 200 mg/L was about 1000, 1250, and 1030 Ω•cm2 at 0.5, 1 and 2 h, respectively. The electrical equivalent circuit was the same as Fig. 4, and the fitting parameter was shown in Table 1. For Rp value, with the increase of reaction time, the Rp value firstly increased and then decreased at the same N-CDs content, and reached the highest value in HCl solution after adding N-CDs prepared at 1 h. By analysis, the Rp value of steel at 200 mg/L was about 992.5, 1265.1, and 1051.2 Ω•cm2 at 0.5, 1 and 2 h, respectively. For double-layer capacitance, the change trend was opposite to Rp value, that is, the Cdl value firstly decreased and then increased as N-CDs content increased. Through observation, the Cdl value of steel at 200 mg/L was about 38.9, 21.2, and 29.2 μF•cm−2 at 0.5, 1 and 2 h, which was 1 ~ 2 orders of magnitude lower than that in blank solution. In terms of deviation index, the variation trend was similar with the Rp value, indicating that the roughness of corrosion interface was the lowest after adding N-CDs prepared at 1 h.

The potentiodynamic polarization test of steel in HCl solution were examined at 25 °C in the absence and presence of N-CDs inhibitor. Fig. 6 showed the tafel curves after soaking in N-CDs solution for 1 day. Compared with Fig. S2 , the polarization curves transferred to a more negative direction after adding N-CDs as a whole. The potentiodynamic factors such as corrosion potential (Ecorr), icorr, Rp as well as θ and IE were summarized in Table 2 . For corrosion current density, the icorr value of steel in blank solution was about 326 μA•cm−2, which was obviously higher than that in N-CDs solution. The icorr value presented a downtrend and even decreased by an order of magnitude as N-CDs content increased, this may be attributed to the accumulation of N-CDs on the substrate surface. At the same N-CDs content, the icorr value of steel firstly decreased and then increased as reaction temperature and time increased, which reached the lowest value at 180 °C and 1 h. In the case of polarization resistance, the Rp value of steel in blank solution was about 20.7 Ω•cm2, which was 1 ~ 2 orders of magnitude lower than that in N-CDs solution. Along with increased N-CDs content, the Rp value revealed an uptrend. At 200 mg/L, the Rp value was about 986.9, 1331, and 1153.2 Ω•cm2 at reaction temperature of 200, 180 and 160 °C, respectively. Meanwhile, the Rp value was about 986.9, 1259.4 and 1049.3 Ω•cm2 at reaction time of 0.5, 1 and 2 h, respectively. In terms of surface coverage and inhibition efficiency, the change trend was the same. Along with increased N-CDs content, the θ and IE values were augmented to varying degrees. For example, at reaction temperature and reaction time of 200 °C and 0.5 h, the IE value ranged from 74% at 50 mg/L to 94% at 200 mg/L. As reaction temperature decreased from 200 to 160 °C, the IE value increased from 94% to 96% and then decreased to 95% at N-CDs content of 200 mg/L. At the same time, the reaction time also affected the IE of N-CDs. By comparison, the IE value reached the highest at reaction time of 1 h. Therefore, the reaction parameters of 200 °C and 1 h were the optimum condition. In this case, it could be inferred that the stable N-CDs adsorption film was formed on steel surface, which provided a barrier to the attack of corrosive ions and inhibited corrosion in aggressive solutions. Moreover, as seen in Table S1, compared with other carbon dots-based inhibitors [19–24,35–38], the as-prepared N-CDs inhibitor showed excellent corrosion inhibition ability for metal in acid solution.

SEM technique was utilized to study the 2D surface morphology of steel in the absence and presence of N-CDs after soaking in HCl solution, the result was shown in Fig. 7 . For various N-CDs contents, the reaction conditions of 200 °C and 0.5 h were selected as a representative because the change trend of other conditions was the same. The image of steel surface immersed in HCl solution without inhibitor was presented in Fig. 7 a, which showed a rough and severe corrosion surface. After adding 50 mg/L of N-CDs, as seen in Fig. 7 b, the severity of corrosion and surface roughness were considerably diminished. As N-CDs content increased, the corrosion area and corrosion degree were reduced, implying that the corrosive effect was gradually weakened (Fig. 7 b ~ e). In terms of reaction temperature, as reaction temperature decreased from 200 to 180 °C, the steel surface became smoother and the number of pits and cracks was reduced (Fig. 7 f). Slight corrosion was observed as reaction temperature further increased (Fig. 7 g). For reaction time, in the presence of 200 mg/L N-CDs, the corrosion situation firstly weakened and then aggravated with the increase of reaction time as a whole. SEM result presented a relatively clean and smooth metal surface when the reaction time was 1 h (Fig. 7 h).

3D surface morphology and surface toughness of steel after 1 day soaking in HCl solution with and without N-CDs were obtained by LSCM technique, which was shown in Fig. 8 . The surface morphology of steel in blank solution revealed obvious bulge due to the attack of corrosion ions and the surface roughness was about 0.832 μm (Fig. 8 a). After adding 50 mg/L of N-CDs, as seen in Fig. 8 b, the surface roughness was greatly reduced (0.525 μm), which proved that the N-CDs was significantly slowed down the corrosion. As N-CDs content increased, the surface roughness showed a decreasing trend, suggesting that the thick absorption film of N-CDs was gradually formed (Fig. 8 b ~ e). For reaction temperature, the surface roughness of steel after soaking was about 0.247, 0.211 and 0.231 μm at reaction temperature of 200, 180, 160 °C, which was reduced by 70.3%, 74.6% and 72.2% compared with blank specimen (Fig. 8 e ~ g). Thus, the reduction degree of surface roughness was the greatest at reaction temperature of 180 °C. In terms of reaction time, as reaction time increased from 0.5 to 1 h, the surface roughness decreased from 0.247 to 0.226 μm (Fig. 8 h). However, slight rebound was observed as reaction time further increased to 2 h (Fig. 8 i), implying that proper reaction time could optimize the corrosion inhibition performance of N-CDs.

Fig. 9 showed the corrosion rate (Crate) of steel after 1 day soaking in HCl solution with and without N-CDs. At blank solution, the Crate value was 23.75 mm/y in HCl solution. The high Crate value was due to the accelerated degradation of steel in the presence of H+ and Cl-. After adding 50 mg/L of N-CDs, the Crate value decreased to 14.56 mm/y under preparation parameters of 200 °C and 0.5 h, which was 38.1% lower than that of blank specimen, further verifying the corrosion inhibitive ability of N-CDs in HCl solution. As N-CDs content increased, the Crate value displayed a downtrend and reached the minimum at 200 mg/L. Moreover, the reaction temperature had obvious impact on the Crate value of steel. By observation, the Crate value firstly decreased and then rebounded as reaction temperature decreased. At 200 mg/L, the Crate value was 8.18, 4.93, 7.39 mm/y at 200, 180 and 160 °C. Therefore, it showed the lowest Crate value at reaction temperature of 180 °C. In the case of reaction time, the variation trend of Crate value was similar to the effect of reaction temperature and the lowest Crate value was obtained at reaction time of 1 h.

Adsorption isotherm could be selected to reflect the adsorption essence and adsorption strength of inhibitor on metal surface, which was shown in Fig. 10 . Through calculation, the C/θ and C values displayed a good linear relationship. Meanwhile, the adsorption free energy (ΔG0 ads) and adsorption equilibrium constant (K) were obtained. The high adsorption equilibrium constant suggested strong adsorption strength [39]. From the result, the K values of N-CDs prepared at 200, 180 as well as 160 °C were about 48.30, 140.84 and 75.76 L/g, respectively, implying that the adsorption strength of N-CDs prepared at 180 °C was the strongest. In the case of reaction time, the K value firstly increased and then reduced as the increase of reaction time, and reached the maximum at reaction time of 1 h. For ΔG0 ads value, when the value of ΔG0 ads was greater than −20 kJ mol−1, the main process contained physical adsorption. When the value of ΔG0 ads was less than −40 kJ mol−1, it mainly involved chemical adsorption [40]. By observation, the ΔG0 ads value of N-CDs prepared at 200 °C-0.5 h, 180 °C-1 h, 160 °C-1 h, 200 °C-1 h, and 200 °C-2 h was about −26.72, −29.37, −27.84, −27.78, and −27.38 kJ mol−1, respectively. These phenomena confirmed that the adsorption essence of all N-CDs was the physical and chemical co-adsorption.

The adsorption layer formation of N-CDs on steel surface could be used to confirm by XPS, which was shown in Fig. 11 . By fitting, the XPS-N1s could be divided into three peaks of N-C, N-Fe and N+H-, which was located at 400, 401.2 and 402.1 eV, respectively [41]. The formation of N-Fe bond validated the physical and chemical co-adsorption of N-CDs on steel surface. Fig. 12 revealed the protection mechanism schematic of N-CDs for steel corrosion in HCl solution. Compared with previous analysis, the N-CDs inhibitor would attach to the steel surface through the reaction between heteroatoms and steel (chemical adsorption). In the meantime, the N-CDs owned several functional groups, such as hydroxyl and carbonyl groups, which played an obvious effect on the adsorption by electrostatic attraction force (physical adsorption). After that, the N-CDs could adsorb on steel surface, which would prevent the metal dissolution at anode and suppress the cathodic evolution of hydrogen. In this case, the N-CDs could reduce the surface active sites and inhibit the corrosion. In addition, the presence of functional groups on the surface of N-CDs may cause the mutual affinity between N-CDs and produced a mass of agglomerates on the surface of steel, thereby resisting corrosive ions. Therefore, the N-CDs could adsorb on steel surface to form the protective film and then prevented the corrosion medium from the direct contact with steel.

Through high temperature cracking process, some N-CDs inhibitors were prepared at various reaction temperatures (200, 180, 160 °C) and reaction time (0.5, 1, 2 h). A series of detection equipment were chosen to analyze the structure and corrosion inhibition ability in HCl solution. By analysis, the reaction temperature and reaction time greatly affect the corrosion inhibition property of N-CDs. The icorr was the lowest and the Rp, θ and IE were the highest at preparation parameters of 180 °C and 1 h. Meanwhile, the IE exceeded to 90% at N-CDs content of 200 mg/L, revealing a super inhibitive effect for steel in corrosion solution. From XPS and adsorption isotherm results, the adsorption essence of N-CDs was the physical and chemical co-adsorption.

Jiaxin Luo is responsible for Writing - original draft; Xiong Cheng, Chaofa Zhong, Xinhua Chen are responsible for Data curation and Writing - review & editing; Y.W. Ye, H. Zhao, H. Chen are responsible for Supervision, Validation and Funding acquisition. All data are authentic and reliable. It has not been published in other journals or submitted for publication.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Scientific Research Foundation of Jiangxi University of Science and Technology (No. 205200100421); Jiangxi Provincial Department of Education (No. GJJ190469); Program of Qingjiang Excellent Young Talents, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology (JXUSTQJYX2020010); Distinguished Professor Program of Jinggang Scholars in institutions of higher learning, Jiangxi Province; Jiangxi Province “Double Thousand Plan” Science and Technology Innovation high-end Talent Project (jxsq2019201039); Jiangxi Province “Double Thousand Plan” Innovation Leading Talent Short-term Project (jxsq2020007).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2021.116783.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
